content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 11, 2000

Ann M. Siprelle
Kronick, Moskovitz, Tiedemann & Girard
400 Capitol Mall, 27th Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814-4417

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-213

Dear Ms. Siprelle:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Woodland City
Councilmember Jeff Monroe and Woodland Tree Commissioner Terri Jessen concerning their
duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions¹ of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").²

QUESTION

May Councilmember Monroe and Commissioner Jessen participate in the deliberations
and vote on the Sycamore Ranch Community Facilities District No.1, Phase 2 Roadway and
Utility Improvements Project despite the fact that both officials own homes that are within 300
feet of one of the streets that will be improved under the plan?

CONCLUSION

Both Councilmember Monroe and Commissioner Jessen may participate in the
deliberations and vote on the Sycamore Ranch Community Facilities District No.1, Phase 2
Roadway and Utility Improvements Project so long as the decision will have no financial effect

¹ Please note that the Commission is currently considering various amendments to the conflict-of-interest regulations
discussed below. You may wish to monitor the progress of these amendments on the Commission's website
(www.fppc.ca.gov).
² Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of
the California Code of Regulations.

Our File No. A-00-213
Page 2

on the value of their respective properties. Reliance on an appraisal immunizes the official only
to the extent that such reliance is reasonable at the time of the decision.

FACTS

The city is currently considering the Sycamore Ranch Community Facilities District
No.1, Phase 2 Roadway and Utility Improvements Project (the Sycamore Ranch Project or the
"project"). The project was initially proposed as a part of the Sycamore Ranch Area Specific
Plan which was approved in 1990. The Specific Plan provides, in pertinent part:

"Gibson Road is classified as an arterial street, and is intended to
carry relatively large volumes of traffic without conflict with uses on
abutting properties.... Sensitive uses, such as residential development
would be shielded from road noise by sound walls, berms or other noise
attenuation provisions....

"The function of Gibson Road will be to serve as a major east-west
roadway linking the southern tier of Woodland to the west with State
Highway 113, and with Road 102, and, via Road 102 with Interstate 5.
Gibson Road/Road 102 together are likely to serve as a by-pass route
serving through-traffic transferring between State Highway 113 and
Interstate 5 in two directions....

"The adverse effects stemming from acceptance of this by-pass
route function can be alleviated by implementing protective measures
including sound walls, berms landscaping (including minimum setback to
the soundwall to permit the installation of a planting strip), street trees,
undergrounding of utilities and limitations on the number and design of
intersections."

The Sycamore Ranch Project includes the following: reconstruction to four lane arterial
cross section (including the addition of curbs, gutters and sidewalks on the north side, a raised
medium, and additional pavement); the installation of street lights, landscaping for the medium
and adjacent to existing sound walls, modifying various traffic signals, relocation of the signal
controller, and installation of a new water line to serve the County Sheriff's facility.

In addition, as part of the project, the City Tree Commission will be considering the
treatment of the olive trees along Gibson Road. The removal of the trees might affect public
views along the road and could alter shade/shadow patterns. Mitigation options to be considered
as part of the plan include: (a) plant new, different trees; (b) realign the road to save more
existing trees; (c) remove and donate for relocation; (d) replace all the olive trees.

Our File No. A-00-213
Page 3

Councilmember Monroe and Commissioner Jessen own residences within 300 feet of
Gibson Road. The homes are not on Gibson Road but back up to Gibson Road. The backs of the
homes are separated from Gibson Road by previously installed sound walls. According to the
appraisal you provided, "[t]here is no ingress or egress from Gibson Road to the subject tract of
homes. Access is from Pioneer Avenue et Co. Rd. 101 which are one block north of Gibson
Road."

ANALYSIS

Step One: Are the individuals "public officials?"

As a city councilmember in Woodland, Councilmember Monroe is a "member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency" and, therefore, is a "public
official" subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; Reg. 18701(a).)
Tree Commissioner Jessen is also a public official since Commissioner Jessen is on a decision-
making commission of the city.

Step Two: Are the public officials making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Reg. 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decision-
maker regarding the governmental decision. (Reg. 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the
official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her
agency. (Reg. 18702.3.)

Your question concerns whether the councilmember may deliberate and vote on the
Sycamore Ranch Improvements Project. The deliberation and vote is considered making and
participating in making a governmental decision. Similarly, the commissioner will be
considering what action to take, if any, with respect to olive trees bordering the roadways in the
plan. For example, with respect to the project, the commission will discuss and decide whether
the city should remove the trees. This is also considered making and participating in making a
governmental decision.

Step Three: Do the public officials have economic interests?

Under section 87103 of the Act, there are six different types of economic interests that
may result in a conflict of interest for a public official. Your question concerns real property

Our File No. A-00-213
Page 4

owned by the councilmember and real property owned by the commissioner. A public official
has an economic interest in any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect
interest worth $1,000 or more in fair market value. (Section 87103(b); Reg. 18703.2.) We
assume that both officials have an interest in their respective residences worth more than $1,000.

Step Four: Are the public officials' economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?

Real property is directly involved in a decision when the decision involves: (1) the
zoning, sale, lease, or boundary change of the property; (2) a license, permit or other land use
entitlement authorizing a specific use of the property; (3) taxes or fees to be assessed on the
property; or (4) redevelopment, and the property is located in the redevelopment area. (Reg.
18704.2(a).) If real property is not directly involved in a decision, it is indirectly involved for
purposes of finding the applicable materiality standard. (Reg. 18704.2(b).) In this case, none of
the "directly involved" standards apply. Thus, under your facts, both officials own real property
that is indirectly involved in the decisions concerning the proposed project.

Step Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on the public officials' economic
interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?

Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official must
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one of those economic interests. This determination takes two steps. First, the official
must find and apply the applicable materiality standard set forth in Commission regulations.
(Reg. 18700(b)(5), Regulation 18705, et seq.) After finding the applicable materiality standard,
the official must then decide whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met.
(Reg. 18700(b)(6).)

An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially likely."
(Reg. 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a
governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made is highly situation-
specific. A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On
the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

Regulation 18705.2 sets forth the materiality standards that apply to economic interests in
real property. The materiality thresholds vary depending upon the distance between the official's
real property and the real property that is the subject of the decision. Under the regulation, the
effect of a decision on real property is material if the real property in which an official has a
direct, indirect, or beneficial ownership interest is located within a 300-foot radius of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the decision,
unless the decision will have no financial effect upon the official's real property interest.

Our File No. A-00-213
Page 5

Thus, the question is: under the test applicable to the real property of the public officials
in question, whether the decisions are substantially likely to have any financial effect on the
value of the real property. Only if no financial effect is foreseeable will the officials be able to
participate in the decisions.

Also of note in this analysis is the fact that the project in question was previously
approved in 1990 as part of the specific plan for that area. The specific plan called for the
implementation of protective measures on Gibson Road to alleviate adverse effects caused by
potential increased traffic. In the policy we have advised that "implementation" decisions, which
effectuate, but do not change, the policies established in the specific plan may be analyzed
independently from the specific plan as a whole, so long as the current decisions do not affect the
specific plan as a whole. Thus, where the decisions are "implementation" decisions, the question
is whether the implementation decision will have an independent material financial effect on the
economic interest of the official, irrespective of financial effects caused by the specific plan
decisions that have already been made. (Talley Advice Letter, No. A-96-123.)

You have provided an appraisal of the possible effects on the real property of the
officials. The letter from the appraiser states that the appraiser considered the effects on traffic,
noise, air quality, and view in the officials' neighborhood in arriving at his conclusion.³ The
appraiser concluded that the project would not have any financial effect on the value of the real
property of the officials. He noted that both parcels are within 300 feet of Gibson Road, but are
not situated on Gibson Road. Rather, the lots backup to Gibson Road and are separated from
Gibson Road by a soundwall. Further, there is no access to the officials' neighborhood directly
from Gibson Road. Further, the appraiser specific analyzed the effect of both the general road
decisions and the effect of the removal of the olive trees in reaching his conclusion.

The appraiser concluded that any financial effect on the real property of the officials
occurred when the project was identified in the specific plan, and not in the implementation of
the specific plan. The appraisal provides "[u]nder the proposed project, the objectives of the
[specific plan] relevant to circulation and utility infrastructure would remain the same as those
previously identified in the [specific plan] environmental impact report. The current project is
the implementation of the road widening and the utility system that will serve the Sycamore
Ranch Project." In other words, when considered independently, the implementation of the
specific plan will have no reasonable foreseeable financial effects on the fair market value of the
officials' real property.

Please note, that our advice is limited to the facts presented. (Section 83114.)
Ultimately, the public official with the potential conflict of interest bears the responsibility of
deciding whether a material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable. Evidence that there will
be no financial effect on real property may take the form of an independent appraisal that takes
into account the factors listed above and omits no other pertinent factor. Such an appraisal is

³ As noted above, both officials live in the same neighborhood.

Our File No. A-00-213
Page 6

appropriate evidence on which to rely when determining whether there will be no financial effect
on the real property. The actual sufficiency of any appraisal is, of course, a question of fact that
the Commission cannot decide. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Reliance on an
appraisal immunizes the official only to the extent that such reliance is reasonable at the time of
the decision.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
